findings.title: Public launch on Spotify is structurally impossible under Feb-2026 quota rules — Extended Quota now needs a registered business + 250k MAU (org-only), Dev Mode is non-commercial + 5 users
findings.rule: Spotify 'Quota modes' + 'Update on Developer Access and Platform Security' (2026-02-06): Development Mode 'supports learning, experimentation, and personal projects for non-commercial use by individual developers' and is capped at 5 authenticated users; Extended Quota Mode (the only mode allowing unlimited public users) requires a legally registered business, ~250,000 monthly active users, availability in key markets, an active launched service, and 'as of May 15th 2025, Spotify only accepts applications from organizations (not individuals).'
findings.currentBehavior: The entire Spotify integration (OAuth connect in backend/app/services/spotify.js:48-64, playback via Web Playback SDK + App Remote, profile build, Like, session playlist) is architected as the app's PRIMARY playback engine for a to-be-store-published app owned by an individual developer (Daniel Malede) with 0 launched MAU. Only Development Mode is attainable, which permits ≤5 users and is explicitly non-commercial.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/spotify.js:5-64 (OAuth + full scope set built for public users); backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:82-144 (connect/callback wired for arbitrary end users); memory 'source_sink_architecture_requirements' = Spotify is the playback sink. Live source: Spotify Quota modes doc (Extended Quota = registered business + 250k MAU, org-only since 2025-05-15) and the 2026-02-06 platform-security blog (Dev Mode = non-commercial, individual, 5-user cap).
findings.remediation: HALT store submission of any build that depends on Spotify serving real end users. Escalate as a Pause & Guide business/legal decision: either (a) form a registered business + apply for Extended Quota knowing the 250k-MAU 'chicken-and-egg' gate will likely reject a pre-launch app, or (b) treat Spotify as a personal/dev-only feature and do not ship it as a public store feature.
findings.cleanAlternative: Ship the store app with the already-built Spotify-independent vector Live Discovery + a non-Spotify playback sink (the 'source ⊥ sink' architecture already in-repo) as the public path; keep Spotify strictly behind a dev/beta gate limited to ≤5 Premium testers until/unless Extended Quota is granted.

findings.title: Session-playlist transport calls a removed endpoint — POST /users/{id}/playlists ('Create Playlist for user') was deleted in the Feb-2026 Web API changelog
findings.rule: Spotify Web API Changelog — February 2026: the 'Create Playlist for user' endpoint (POST /users/{user_id}/playlists) was removed for Development Mode apps; playlist creation is available for the current user only via POST /me/playlists.
findings.currentBehavior: createSessionPlaylist POSTs to /users/{me.id}/playlists to build the hidden 'Kokonada Session' playlist that is the App-Remote playback context, rewritten every generation. This hits a now-removed endpoint (Spotify postponed the cutover for existing integrations, so it is a latent break + non-compliant call, not yet a hard 404).
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/spotifySessionPlaylist.js:30-37 (`axios.post(\`${BASE_API}/users/${encodeURIComponent(me.id)}/playlists\`, ...)`), called from writeSessionPlaylist:59-84 and the biometric generation path. Live source: Spotify Feb-2026 changelog + migration guide (POST /users/{id}/playlists removed → use POST /me/playlists).
findings.remediation: Migrate createSessionPlaylist to POST /me/playlists (drops the need to first GET /me and the /users/{id} path). Confirm via the Spotify dashboard whether this Client ID is grandfathered; do not rely on the postponement.
findings.cleanAlternative: Use POST /me/playlists for the current user; or eliminate the app-managed playlist transport entirely and drive App Remote via an in-memory queue, removing the playlist-modify-private scope dependency.

findings.title: Spotify integration now requires every user (and the app owner) to hold Spotify Premium — collides with the 'app is 100% free' locked decision
findings.rule: Spotify Quota modes + 2026-02-06 platform-security update: 'All Development Mode apps require the app owner to have an active Spotify Premium subscription. If the owner's Premium subscription lapses, the app will stop working.' Premium requirement for Dev Mode API access takes effect 2026-03-09. Web Playback SDK independently requires Premium (account_error otherwise).
findings.currentBehavior: The app requests the `streaming` scope and initializes the Web Playback SDK for all Spotify users; playback silently fails for non-Premium accounts (account_error), and post-2026-03-09 even data/API calls require the token owner to be Premium. This is not surfaced to users as a hard requirement and contradicts the free-product positioning.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/spotify.js:37 (`'streaming'` scope); frontend/src/services/spotifyPlayer.ts:89-97 (initialization_error/account_error 'Spotify Premium required'); frontend/src/hooks/useSpotifyPlayer.ts:46-74 (SDK init for any spotify user). Live source: Spotify Quota modes ('Development Mode apps require the app owner to have Premium') + 2026-02-06 blog (Premium mandatory from 2026-03-09).
findings.remediation: Do not present Spotify playback as available to all users. Gate the Spotify path behind an explicit 'requires Spotify Premium' disclosure, and reconcile with the KOKONADA_SECURITY_DATA_AUDIT 'zero-cost' decision — a Premium dependency means the core biometric-music loop is not free for Spotify users.
findings.cleanAlternative: Route the free, public experience through the Spotify-independent playback sink; reserve the Spotify Premium sink for users who opt in and already hold Premium, with the requirement stated up front.

findings.title: Spotify Content/metadata rendered during playback WITHOUT the required Spotify attribution/logo/link on the mini-player and LivePlayer
findings.rule: Spotify Design & Branding Guidelines / Developer Policy: 'If you display any Spotify Content you must clearly attribute the content as being supplied and made available by Spotify, by using the Spotify Marks. Metadata, cover art ... must be accompanied by a link back to the applicable ... content ... on the Spotify Service' and for a Streaming SDA 'there shall be no playback of Spotify Content without showing relevant cover art and metadata.'
findings.currentBehavior: During live Spotify playback the persistent mini-player and the LivePlayer render the Spotify-sourced track title + artist with NO Spotify logo, mark, or 'Listen on Spotify' link. Attribution exists only on the full Now Playing screen. Every surface that shows Spotify metadata must carry the mark + link back.
findings.evidence: frontend/src/components/NowPlayingBar.tsx:43-44 (`{track.title}` / `{track.artist}` — no attribution); frontend/src/components/LivePlayer/LivePlayer.tsx:32-33 (title/artist, no mark); contrast frontend/src/pages/NowPlayingPage.tsx:218-228 which DOES attribute ('Listen on Spotify' + logo + open.spotify.com link). Live source: Spotify Design guidelines / Developer Policy attribution clause (metadata must be accompanied by the Spotify brand and link back).
findings.remediation: Add the Spotify mark + a 'Listen on Spotify' deep link to open.spotify.com on every surface that displays Spotify track metadata during playback (NowPlayingBar, LivePlayer, history, any queue/up-next list). The mini-player should also show cover art per the Streaming-SDA 'no playback without cover art and metadata' rule.
findings.cleanAlternative: Centralize a `<SpotifyAttribution track>` component (logo + 'Listen on Spotify' link) and require it wherever a Spotify-sourced track title/artist/art is rendered; get a designer SHIP before merging.

findings.title: Spotify logo is a hand-recreated SVG path and used icon-only where the full logo (icon + wordmark) is required
findings.rule: Spotify Design & Branding Guidelines: in partner integrations 'you should always use the full logo (icon + wordmark). Only the icon is allowed if it's featured as an app icon on the app screen of a device.' Spotify provides official downloadable logo assets and prohibits recreating/modifying the mark (recreation clause UNVERIFIED against the live assets page — treat as precautionary).
findings.currentBehavior: SpotifyLogo.tsx redraws the Spotify mark from a bespoke `<path>` (not an official Spotify-supplied asset) and it is used as an icon-only mark next to 'Listen on Spotify' rather than the full icon+wordmark logo.
findings.evidence: frontend/src/components/SpotifyLogo.tsx:3-9 (custom `<svg><path d=... fill="#1DB954"/>`); used at frontend/src/pages/NowPlayingPage.tsx:226 (`<SpotifyLogo className="size-4" /> Listen on Spotify`). Live source: Spotify Design guidelines (full logo requirement, official assets).
findings.remediation: Replace the recreated path with the official downloadable Spotify logo asset, honor clear-space/min-size, and use the full logo (icon + wordmark) in attribution contexts. Do not recolor. VERIFY the exact recreation/asset rule against developer.spotify.com/documentation/design before shipping.
findings.cleanAlternative: Ship the official Spotify green full-logo SVG from Spotify's brand asset pack and reference it wherever attribution is shown.

findings.title: Over-broad OAuth scopes — user-read-email and user-read-private are requested but the fetched profile (incl. email) is never stored or used
findings.rule: Spotify Developer Policy / Compliance Tips (least privilege): request only the scopes your integration actually uses; requesting access to user data you do not use is disfavored and is a review/rejection risk during quota vetting. (Cross-checked against the app's own 'least privilege' locked decision.)
findings.currentBehavior: The scope set requests user-read-email and user-read-private, but getProfile()'s return value ({spotifyId, displayName, email}) is discarded by the only caller — it is called purely to 'verify token works.' No user email, country, or product field is ever persisted or read. These two scopes grant access to personal data the app never uses.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/spotify.js:12-13 (`'user-read-private','user-read-email'`); getProfile at spotify.js:193-199 returns email; sole caller backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:106 (`await spotify.getProfile(tokens.accessToken); // verify token works`) discards the result; no `.email`/`spotifyId` from the user profile is stored (grep shows spotifyId usage is only track-feature related).
findings.remediation: Drop user-read-email (definitively unused — email is discarded) and re-verify user-read-private is unnecessary (display_name/id return without it); if kept, document the exact field consumed. Fewer scopes also means the client-exposed access token (below) carries less authority.
findings.cleanAlternative: Trim SCOPES to only what is exercised (playback, library read/modify, playlist-modify-private, top/recent/library-read); remove email/private personal-data scopes entirely.

findings.title: Full-scope Spotify access token (incl. library + playlist WRITE) is handed to browser JavaScript for the Web Playback SDK, which only needs `streaming`
findings.rule: Least-privilege / secure-token-handling: a token exposed to the client should carry only the authority the client needs. The Web Playback SDK's getOAuthToken requires only `streaming`, but Spotify issues one token bearing ALL granted scopes, so the browser receives write access to the user's Liked Songs and playlists.
findings.currentBehavior: GET /api/integrations/spotify/token returns the user's decrypted access token to the SPA; that token also carries user-library-modify and playlist-modify-private. Any XSS or malicious extension in the web app could silently modify the user's Spotify library/playlists, not just stream.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:185-202 (`res.json({ access_token: accessToken })`); consumed at frontend/src/hooks/useSpotifyPlayer.ts:11-31 + frontend/src/services/spotifyPlayer.ts:74 (`getOAuthToken`). Write scopes minted at backend/app/services/spotify.js:27-36.
findings.remediation: Keep all Spotify write operations (Like, session playlist) strictly server-side (they already have dedicated backend endpoints) and mint a narrowed token for the browser if/when Spotify supports per-request scope downscoping; at minimum, tighten frontend CSP and treat the token as high-value. Reducing the granted scope set (finding above) directly shrinks this blast radius.
findings.cleanAlternative: Never expose write-capable Spotify tokens to the client — proxy playback control through the backend, or issue the SDK a token from a separate Client that only holds `streaming`.

findings.title: Dead/deprecated Spotify endpoints still wired (audio-features, recommendations, bulk /artists) — wasted calls, rate-budget burn, and a dead code path
findings.rule: Spotify deprecated /audio-features and /recommendations for new apps (Nov 2024); the Feb-2026 changelog further deprecated bulk-metadata/top-track endpoints. Calling them is not a ban, but they 403/404 for this app and the code still ships them (getTopTrackFeatures appears entirely unused/dead).
findings.currentBehavior: getTopTrackFeatures and batchAudioFeatures call /audio-features; getRecommendations calls /recommendations (with a search fallback); getArtistsGenres calls bulk /artists behind a 403-cache. getTopTrackFeatures is exported but has no production caller.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/spotify.js:202-219 (getTopTrackFeatures → /audio-features, no caller), :397-416 (batchAudioFeatures → /audio-features), :427-462 (getRecommendations → /recommendations), :295-324 (getArtistsGenres → bulk /artists with _artistGenres403Until cache). Confirmed dead per the code's own comments (spotify.js:221-224, 451-458).
findings.remediation: Remove getTopTrackFeatures (dead) and any remaining calls to /audio-features and /recommendations; rely solely on listening-history + the internal feature store (ReccoBeats/LLM). This also trims the client-exposed scope surface and avoids burning the reduced Dev-Mode rate budget.
findings.cleanAlternative: Delete the deprecated-endpoint wrappers and keep only the listening-history readers (top/recent/library/playlists) plus the app's own feature/embedding store.

findings.title: Gemini free-tier fallback egresses Garmin/HealthKit biometrics + free-text notes to Google for model training and human review
findings.currentBehavior: _generate() only sends to Groq when LLM_API_KEY/GROQ_API_KEY is set; otherwise it silently falls back to the Gemini SDK with GEMINI_API_KEY. The emotion prompt embeds HR/HRV/SpO2/body-battery/readiness/sleep and the user's raw free-text note.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/geminiEngine.js:46-91 (Groq-or-Gemini fallback; lines 88-90 call getModel().generateContent on GEMINI_API_KEY); prompt built with biometrics at geminiEngine.js:231-255 and injected at :286, free-text note at :284. VERIFIED clause: Google's Gemini API unpaid/free tier states prompts and responses are used to 'provide, improve, and develop' Google products and may be reviewed by humans; only the PAID tier excludes training, and EEA/UK/CH clients must use paid services (ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/billing; Gemini API Additional Terms). Sending special-category health data (GDPR Art. 9) to a training-eligible, human-reviewed endpoint is a data-leak/consent violation.
findings.remediation: Delete the Gemini fallback branch (geminiEngine.js:88-90) OR require a billed Gemini tier with a signed DPA and never route health/free-text prompts through a free key. Fail closed (throw) if no Groq key rather than silently degrading to a training-eligible endpoint.
findings.cleanAlternative: Groq-only inference with zero-data-retention enabled; if Groq is unconfigured, fall back to the deterministic mood/BPM path (applyMoodFallback) — never to a free LLM that trains on inputs.

findings.title: Groq receives special-category health data + free-text without a confirmed DPA / zero-data-retention
findings.currentBehavior: Every emotion generation POSTs HR/HRV/SpO2/sleep + the user's free-text note to api.groq.com. Comment at geminiEngine.js:336-341 asserts prompts are 'anonymised', but health data + free-text is still personal (and special-category) data.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/geminiEngine.js:33,49 (baseUrl api.groq.com/openai/v1), :286 (biometrics), :284 (free-text); backend/app/services/llmClient.js:33,39-51. VERIFIED clause: Groq does NOT train on Inputs/Outputs by default and does not retain by default, BUT keeps temporary input/output logs up to 30 days for abuse/troubleshooting unless the Console 'zero data retention' setting is enabled; processing is governed by the Groq Services Agreement + DPA, not the consumer privacy policy (console.groq.com/docs/your-data; console.groq.com/docs/legal/customer-data-processing-addendum).
findings.remediation: Execute the Groq DPA, enable zero-data-retention for the account, and disclose Groq as a health-data sub-processor in the privacy policy. Minimize/omit raw free-text egress.
findings.cleanAlternative: Enable Groq ZDR and strip the free-text note (or extract only a coarse mood token server-side) before egress so no reversible health/PII leaves the backend.

findings.title: Garmin Health API called against PRODUCTION endpoints without confirmed program approval — dev-account/API termination risk
findings.currentBehavior: garminCallback exchanges the code, verifies the token, and immediately kicks a 6-month historical backfill against apis.garmin.com. MEMORY notes the integration 'needs Garmin Health API approval'.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/wearable/garmin.js:8 (API_BASE https://apis.garmin.com/wellness-api/rest), :118-133 (requestSixMonthBackfill), backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:475-512 (callback + setImmediate backfill). VERIFIED clause: Garmin Health API production access requires a MANUAL Garmin review (security-compliance vetting) and is gated behind the partner program (developer.garmin.com/gc-developer-program/health-api/; Garmin Connect Developer Program Agreement). Program approvals are reportedly on hold. Hitting production Health API without approval breaches the Program Agreement and risks suspension/termination of API access.
findings.remediation: HALT: keep all Garmin Health API calls behind a disabled flag until written production approval is granted. Do not ship garminConnect/garminCallback/backfill to prod. Confirm approval status with Garmin (Pause & Guide).
findings.cleanAlternative: Ship with the already-built, non-approval-gated Health Connect (Android) / HealthKit push bridge as the biometric source (appleHealth.js + POST /health/batch) until Garmin production access is granted.

findings.title: No 'Garmin [device]' / 'Powered by Garmin' attribution on any screen that displays Garmin-sourced biometrics
findings.currentBehavior: The Now Playing screen shows a live '♥ BPM' badge sourced from Garmin, and the Profile 'health vault' shows Garmin metrics, but a repo-wide search finds zero Garmin attribution/logo. The only 'Powered by' strings are Spotify and the RN launch screen.
findings.evidence: frontend/src/pages/NowPlayingPage.tsx:233 (♥ BPM badge from Garmin HR); mobile ProfileScreen only labels 'Garmin' as text; repo-wide grep 'Powered by Garmin|GarminLogo' → no matches. VERIFIED clause: Garmin API Brand Guidelines require a 'Garmin [device model]' attribution on ALL displays of Garmin device-sourced data (heart rate, Body Battery, etc.) across detailed views, dashboards/summary cards, secondary screens, and exported/derived data; non-compliance may result in suspension or termination of API access (developer.garmin.com/brand-guidelines/api-brand-guidelines/).
findings.remediation: Add 'Garmin [device model]' attribution wherever Garmin-sourced HR/HRV/SpO2/sleep is displayed (Now Playing HR badge, Profile health vault, session history) and on any exported/derived data.
findings.cleanAlternative: Wrap Garmin-sourced metric displays in a single component that always renders the required Garmin attribution string, so no screen can show Garmin data without the mark.

findings.title: Garmin disconnect neither deletes stored health data nor deregisters the user; no deregistration/permission webhook
findings.currentBehavior: garminDisconnect only nulls wearableProvider/wearableToken/garminUserId. The user's already-ingested Garmin BiometricLog/MedicalProfile rows remain. There is no handler for Garmin's user-deregistration/permission-change ping, and no call to delete data Garmin-side.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:514-522 (disconnect nulls fields only); :529-556 (garminWebhook ingests summaries but has no deregistration/permission-change branch). VERIFIED clause: Garmin's Program terms require respecting the user's right to disconnect AND delete their data at any time; retention is only for the duration the end-user maintains the account or until they exercise deletion (Garmin Connect Developer Program Agreement; developer.garmin.com/gc-developer-program/health-api/).
findings.remediation: On disconnect, purge Garmin-sourced BiometricLog/MedicalProfile rows for the user and call Garmin's user-deregistration; implement the Garmin deregistration/permission webhook to purge data when the user revokes on Garmin's side.
findings.cleanAlternative: Reuse the existing GDPR erasure cascade (backend/app/services/privacy/erasure.js) scoped to provider='garmin' on disconnect, plus wire the deregistration ping to the same cascade.

findings.title: YouTube search.list (100 quota units) fires on every YouTube-source generation — daily-quota exhaustion / API suspension risk
findings.currentBehavior: For YouTube-source users, each biometric/emotion generation calls youtube.searchRecommendations, which hits GET /search (the single most expensive endpoint).
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/youtube.js:290-304 (searchRecommendations → GET /youtube/v3/search); backend/app/sockets/biometricHandler.js:572-575 (wired as fetchTracks for YouTube source). VERIFIED clause: search.list costs 100 units; the default per-project quota is 10,000 units/day (~100 searches/day across ALL users). YouTube API Services Developer Policies allow throttling/suspension for exceeding quota or abusive automated usage (developers.google.com/youtube/terms/developer-policies; quota is documented in the Data API cost table).
findings.remediation: Cache/throttle discovery searches, prefer the internal vector corpus (default-on), and file a quota-increase (audited) request before scaling YouTube-source users. Never let search.list run on every generation unbounded.
findings.cleanAlternative: Use YouTube purely as a first-party taste import; drive discovery from the provider-agnostic mbid vector corpus (discoveryVectorService) instead of live search.list.

findings.title: YouTube user data (titles/channels) sent to Groq classifier; verify ≤30-day retention/refresh and service-provider terms for stored YouTube data
findings.currentBehavior: The music/non-music classifier sends YouTube video titles + channel names to Groq for the ambiguous residue. YouTube liked/playlist/subscription/topic data is pulled and folded into the stored MusicProfile/AudioFeature store.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/musicClassifier.js:9-10,64-70 (Groq receives YouTube titles/channels); backend/app/services/youtube.js:138-279 (paginate liked/playlist/subscriptions/topics stored to profile). VERIFIED clause: YouTube API Services ToS caps storage of Non-Authorized Data at 30 calendar days (then delete or refresh), and API Client data given to third parties must stay within service-provider bounds; Authorized Data must be deleted when the user revokes (developers.google.com/youtube/terms/api-services-terms-of-service). Disconnect DOES purge the YouTube library (integrationsController.js:413-449) — good — but corpus/AudioFeature persistence and the Groq egress must be confirmed.
findings.remediation: Confirm no YouTube-derived rows persist >30 days without refresh in shared stores; confirm Groq is bound as a disclosed service provider and document the flow. Extend the caption-service §II standard (keep title/artist out of AI) to the classifier where feasible.
findings.cleanAlternative: Classify YouTube tracks with the deterministic signals already implemented (category/topic/channel/lexicon) and drop the Groq title tie-breaker; expire stored YouTube-derived rows on a ≤30-day cycle.

findings.title: YouTube taste data drives playlists played back on Spotify — 'substantially similar / substitute service' and cross-service data-use scrutiny
findings.currentBehavior: YouTube-sourced candidates are resolved to Spotify URIs and played through Spotify's SDK; YouTube search results effectively feed a competitor's playback.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/crossPlatform.js:57-111 (translateToSpotify resolves youtube tracks to spotify: for playback); fed by youtube.searchRecommendations (biometricHandler.js:572-575). VERIFIED clause: YouTube API Services cannot be used to create a substitute for, or a service substantially similar to, YouTube, and must not move users/data in a way that replicates YouTube's core experience without significant independent value (developers.google.com/youtube/terms/developer-policies).
findings.remediation: Obtain legal/YouTube review of the cross-service flow, or keep YouTube strictly as a first-party library import whose tracks (where surfaced) play on YouTube's own player; avoid using YouTube-derived signals to drive a competitor's playback at scale.
findings.cleanAlternative: Discovery from the provider-agnostic mbid corpus; YouTube limited to optional first-party taste import, decoupled from Spotify playback resolution.

findings.title: iOS login offers Google Sign-In but no Sign in with Apple button — App Store Guideline 4.8 rejection
findings.currentBehavior: The mobile connect/login screen renders only 'Sign in with Google'. The backend already supports Sign in with Apple (/auth/apple + verifyAppleToken), but no Apple button exists in the mobile UI.
findings.evidence: mobile/KokonadaHealth/src/components/ConnectHealthScreen.tsx:157 ('Sign in with Google'); grep across mobile/src for AppleButton/'Sign in with Apple' → no matches; backend support at backend/app/routes/auth.js:10 and authController verifyAppleToken. VERIFIED clause: App Store Review Guideline 4.8 requires any app using a third-party/social login (Google Sign-In) to ALSO offer an equivalent privacy-focused option (limits data to name+email, allows a private/relay email, no ad tracking without consent) — Sign in with Apple qualifies (developer.apple.com/app-store/review/guidelines/ §4.8).
findings.remediation: Add the exact-HIG Sign in with Apple button to the iOS login, wired to the existing /auth/apple endpoint, before any App Store submission.
findings.cleanAlternative: Present Sign in with Apple as an equal option alongside Google on iOS; the backend flow already exists, so only the UI button and native SIWA integration are needed.

findings.title: Garmin webhook authentication is optional and passed in the query string — unauthenticated biometric injection when the secret is unset
findings.currentBehavior: The webhook only checks the secret when GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is set; if the env var is missing it accepts any POST. The secret is a URL query parameter (?secret=), so it lands in access/proxy logs.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:529-533 (guard is `if (process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET && req.query.secret !== ...)` — fails open when unset; secret read from req.query). Contrast the fail-closed Suunto HMAC at backend/app/services/wearable/suunto.js:12-34.
findings.remediation: Fail closed when GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset (reject in production), move the secret out of the query string to a header/HMAC, and verify the payload before ingesting into BiometricLog/MedicalProfile.
findings.cleanAlternative: Adopt the Suunto pattern: HMAC-signature verification over the raw body, refusing all traffic when the secret is unconfigured in production.

findings.title: Spotify-derived artist names/genres are ingested into the LLM (Groq/Gemini) — contradicts Spotify's AI-input prohibition and the app's own caption §II lock
findings.currentBehavior: The emotion prompt lists the user's Spotify-favourite artists as seed_artists and top genres; separately, inferArtistGenres sends up to 200 artist names to Groq. Yet captionService.js was explicitly locked to NEVER send title/artist/genre to the LLM for exactly this reason.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/geminiEngine.js:276,294-296 (seed_artists + top genres in prompt) and :416-419 (batch of artist names to Groq); the compliance lock the rest of the code already applies is documented at backend/app/services/discovery/captionService.js:9-13,66-70. VERIFIED clause: Spotify Developer Policy prohibits using the Spotify Platform or any Spotify Content to 'train a machine learning or AI model or otherwise ingest Spotify Content into a machine learning or AI model' (developer.spotify.com/policy). Enforcement = access revocation of the very Spotify account this audit must protect.
findings.remediation: Stop passing Spotify-originated artist names/genres into the LLM. Derive genres from non-Spotify sources (or precomputed first-party data), and restrict LLM inputs to audio features + emotion signals — the exact standard captionService already enforces.
findings.cleanAlternative: Apply the caption-service pattern app-wide: the LLM sees only audio features + first-party mood/activity/HR context; never artist/title/genre that originated from Spotify Content.

findings.title: Google Play Health Connect: background + history read permissions require the Health Connect declaration, justification, and in-app privacy policy
findings.currentBehavior: The Android app declares sensitive Health Connect permissions including READ_HEALTH_DATA_IN_BACKGROUND and READ_HEALTH_DATA_HISTORY, plus a PermissionsRationaleActivity/privacy-policy rationale (well-structured), but Play Console approval + declaration form + published privacy policy must be confirmed before submission.
findings.evidence: mobile/KokonadaHealth/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml:6-14 (health READ permissions incl. background + history), :70-95 (rationale activity + Android-14 ViewPermissionUsage alias). VERIFIED requirement: Google Play's Health Connect policy requires apps requesting Health Connect permissions to complete the Health Connect declaration, justify each permission (background/history access is high-scrutiny), and provide an accessible privacy policy; non-compliance blocks/removes the listing (support.google.com / Play Console Health Connect policy).
findings.remediation: Complete the Play Console Health Connect declaration, document the necessity of background + history reads, and ensure the linked privacy policy is live and matches actual data use before submission.
findings.cleanAlternative: Request only the minimum Health Connect scopes actually consumed; drop background/history reads unless a shipped feature needs them, reducing review scrutiny.

findings.title: Apple Music 'scaffold' is inert (no MusicKit code) — compliant now, but gate any future MusicKit/branding work
findings.currentBehavior: No MusicKit, music.apple.com, developer-token, or Apple Music provider code exists anywhere in the repo. Apple appears only as HealthKit and Sign in with Apple. So there is no live Apple Music surface to violate MusicKit terms.
findings.evidence: Repo-wide grep 'MusicKit|music.apple.com|developerToken|apple_music|appleMusic' → no matches; Apple references limited to backend/app/services/wearable/appleHealth.js and auth (Sign in with Apple).
findings.remediation: Keep the scaffold unexposed. Before shipping any Apple Music feature, obtain a MusicKit developer token + Apple Music API entitlement and follow the Apple Music Identity Guidelines; do not render an Apple Music logo or 'Apple Music' claim until entitled.
findings.cleanAlternative: Leave Apple Music as an unexposed provider enum with no user-facing mark or API call until MusicKit entitlement + branding review are complete.

findings.title: Special-category biometric data (HR/HRV/SpO2/sleep + inferred mental state) is transmitted to a third-party LLM, directly violating the locked zero-knowledge-biometrics decision
findings.rule: GDPR Art. 9 (processing of special-category health data) + Art. 44 (international transfer) + Art. 28 (sub-processor). Internal: ADR 0005 (locked).
findings.currentBehavior: resolveBiometricContext() decrypts the user's MedicalProfile (restingHR, HRV, respiration, SpO2, bodyBattery, dailyReadiness, sleep stages) and a derived state label, packs it into biometricContext, and passes it to buildEmotionPlaylist. _formatBiometric() renders it as a prompt line ('physiological state "High-Stress / Pre-Panic"; current HR 148 bpm vs resting 52 bpm; HRV 24 ms; SpO2 96%; last sleep 40m deep...') and _generate() POSTs the full prompt to https://api.groq.com/openai/v1/chat/completions (or the Gemini SDK). The biometric-only pipeline (_buildBiometricPrompt) also ships raw live heart rate + activity even when the emotion flag is off.
findings.evidence: geminiEngine.js:231-255 (_formatBiometric builds HR/HRV/SpO2/bodyBattery/readiness/sleep line), :286 (prompt injects 'Last-24h biometric snapshot: ${bio}'), :234 (includes inferred state e.g. 'High-Stress / Pre-Panic'), :305-332 (_buildBiometricPrompt sends 'Heart rate: ${heartRate} bpm'), :46-91 (_generate POSTs prompt to Groq/Gemini), :342-361 (_callGemini). biometricHandler.js:301-351 (resolveBiometricContext decrypts MedicalProfile), :596-604 (passes to buildEmotionPlaylist), :606-614 (adjustBiometricPlaylist sends live HR), :293 (BIO_CONTEXT_ENABLED default ON). CONTRADICTS docs/adr/0005-zero-knowledge-biometrics.md:14-15 'Biometrics are never logged and never shipped to clients or external AI'. Verified Groq: inference not retained/trained-on by default but logged up to 30d for abuse and requires a DPA/ZDR (console.groq.com/docs/your-data).
findings.remediation: HALT the biometric-in-prompt design. Do NOT send raw or lightly-formatted vitals to any external LLM. Keep the physiological classification (computeStateVector, already deterministic + server-side) on-prem and pass only a coarse, non-reversible band/label that is not itself health data, or better, drop biometrics from the prompt entirely and adjust target_bpm/energy locally from the decrypted vitals AFTER the LLM returns taste params. If any egress is retained, it must be gated behind: (a) an executed Groq DPA with Zero-Data-Retention enabled, (b) explicit Art. 9 consent, (c) an EU/adequacy transfer mechanism, (d) privacy-policy + store-form disclosure of the AI sub-processor. Re-affirm ADR 0005 or formally amend it via a new ADR before shipping.
findings.cleanAlternative: Two-stage split: Stage 1 sends ONLY anonymised taste signals + emotion coordinates to the LLM to get seed genres/keywords (no vitals in the prompt). Stage 2 maps decrypted vitals to target_bpm/energy deterministically in worker scope (the state classifier already exists in medicalProfileService.js) — vitals never leave the encrypted server boundary, honoring ADR 0005.

findings.title: Sharing HealthKit/Health-Connect-derived data with third-party AI without disclosure or explicit permission is an App Store rejection risk (Guideline 5.1.3 + new 5.1.2(i) third-party-AI clause)
findings.rule: App Store Review Guidelines 5.1.3 (Health & Health Research) and 5.1.2(i) (Data Use and Sharing — updated Nov 2025 to cover third-party AI).
findings.currentBehavior: Health data ingested from Apple HealthKit (appleHealthPush) and Health Connect (healthBatchIngest) lands in BiometricLog/MedicalProfile, which resolveBiometricContext then feeds into the third-party LLM prompt. No in-app disclosure that health data (or anything derived from it) is shared with an AI provider, and no explicit per-purpose consent is captured for that sharing.
findings.evidence: Ingest → egress chain: integrationsController.js:561-573 (appleHealthPush) + :583-593 (healthBatchIngest) → metricStore.js → MedicalProfile → biometricHandler.js:596-604 → geminiEngine.js:286 external POST. Apple 5.1.3: 'Apps may not use or disclose to third parties data gathered in the health, fitness, and medical research context ... other than improving health management, or for the purpose of health research, and then only with permission.' Apple 5.1.2(i) (Nov-2025 update): apps must clearly disclose where personal data is shared with third parties, including third-party AI, and obtain explicit permission first (developer.apple.com/app-store/review/guidelines/; corroborated TechCrunch 2025-11-13). Music generation is not 'improving health management,' so the carve-out does not apply and permission is mandatory.
findings.remediation: Either remove health data from the third-party-AI path (see finding 1), or add an explicit, separate consent surface naming the AI sub-processor and the exact data shared, and declare the sharing in the App Privacy nutrition label. Do not submit to App Review while HealthKit-derived data reaches an LLM without disclosure + explicit permission — this is a documented rejection/removal trigger.
findings.cleanAlternative: Keep HealthKit-derived vitals fully server-side (no LLM egress). If AI personalization is required, present a dedicated Health-data-sharing consent screen (distinct from the OS HealthKit grant) naming the provider, and mirror it in the nutrition label under 'Data Linked to You → Health' with the third-party-sharing flag set truthfully.

findings.title: Transmitting Garmin-sourced health data to an LLM without fully-informed end-user consent violates the Garmin Connect Developer Program Agreement (developer-account ban risk)
findings.rule: Garmin Connect Developer Program Agreement — no transmitting/sharing end-user data obtained via the API without fully-informed end-user consent; attribution required when Garmin data is shared/exported beyond the app.
findings.currentBehavior: Garmin Health API pushes summaries to the webhook → garminIngest → MedicalProfile/BiometricLog → resolveBiometricContext → LLM prompt. There is no fully-informed consent captured for onward transmission to an AI provider, and no Garmin attribution on the egress.
findings.evidence: integrationsController.js:529-556 (garminWebhook) → garminIngest.js:20-28 → metricStore.js:27-91 → biometricHandler.js:596-604 → geminiEngine.js:286 external POST. Garmin terms (developerportal.garmin.com Garmin Connect Developer Program Agreement): 'Applications ... agree not to transmit or share any end-user data ... obtained through these APIs without first obtaining the fully informed consent of the end user'; commercial data shared/transmitted beyond an application 'must include a Garmin attribution.' Consent for AI egress is not captured anywhere in the connect flow (integrationsController.js:462-511) and no app-level Art.9 consent screen exists.
findings.remediation: Stop routing Garmin-sourced vitals to any external processor, or capture fully-informed, specific consent for that transmission before it occurs and add the required Garmin attribution wherever Garmin-derived data is exported. Confirm the current signed Garmin agreement text (this was verified from the public program agreement — treat the exact executed version as authoritative) before relying on any carve-out.
findings.cleanAlternative: Process Garmin vitals only inside the encrypted server boundary (no third-party egress), removing the consent/attribution obligation entirely; if AI egress is ever needed, gate it behind an explicit consent toggle in the integrations flow plus a visible 'Powered by Garmin' attribution.

findings.title: LLM fallback path can route health-data prompts to Google Gemini's free tier, where inputs are used for product improvement, human review, and model training
findings.rule: Gemini API Additional Terms — unpaid/free tier: content is used to improve Google products and may be human-reviewed; only the paid tier (billing linked) excludes training/human review. GDPR Art. 9.
findings.currentBehavior: _generate() only uses the OpenAI-compatible (Groq) endpoint when LLM_API_KEY/GROQ_API_KEY is set; otherwise it falls back to the Gemini SDK using GEMINI_API_KEY with no tier/paid-account guard. If that key is a free-tier key, the biometric-bearing prompt is used by Google for training and may be seen by human reviewers.
findings.evidence: geminiEngine.js:46-48 (branch on LLM key), :88-90 (Gemini SDK fallback), :34-40 (getModel builds free Gemini client from GEMINI_API_KEY, no paid/ZDR check). Verified: on the unpaid Gemini API, submitted content is used to 'provide, improve, and develop Google products' and may be human-reviewed; paid tier excludes this (ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/billing). Combined with the finding-1 egress, a free-tier key = special-category data used for training.
findings.remediation: Hard-block the free-tier/Gemini path for any prompt that can contain health data: require an allow-listed provider with a signed DPA + no-training guarantee (paid Gemini or Groq ZDR), and fail closed (deterministic local fallback) rather than silently degrading to an untrusted provider. Assert the provider/tier at startup.
findings.cleanAlternative: Remove biometrics from prompts (finding 1). Independently, restrict LLM providers to a vetted allow-list with contractual no-training terms and refuse to boot the biometric pipeline if the configured provider is not on it.

findings.title: Store privacy declarations assert 'no data shared with third parties / health never shared,' contradicting the live LLM egress — a misrepresentation risk on both stores
findings.rule: Accuracy of App Privacy label (Apple) and Data Safety form (Google Play); false/incomplete disclosures are grounds for rejection or removal.
findings.currentBehavior: The drafted declarations tell both stores there are no third-party/AI sub-processors and that health data is never shared, while the code sends health-derived context to Groq/Gemini. The declarations omit the AI sub-processor entirely.
findings.evidence: docs/PRIVACY_DECLARATIONS.md:24 ('no data sold or shared for ads'), :37 ('Health data is never used for advertising, shared with third parties...'), :41 ('Shared with third parties: No'), :52 (Health Connect data 'used solely for on-device→server ... generation and is never shared'). These contradict geminiEngine.js:286/_generate egress and the DATA EGRESS NOTE at geminiEngine.js:336-341 which itself concedes prompts 'are sent to Google Gemini, a third-party sub-processor — document it in the privacy policy / DPA.' The privacy policy/DPA update the note calls for has not been reflected in the declarations.
findings.remediation: Do not submit either store form until the declarations match reality. Either eliminate the egress (then the 'no sharing' claim becomes true) or update the App Privacy label + Data Safety form + privacy policy to name the AI sub-processor, the exact data category shared, and the purpose, with a DPA on file.
findings.cleanAlternative: Resolve finding 1 so the declarations remain accurate as written (health data never leaves the server); keep a standing check that any new external egress forces a declarations diff before submission.

findings.title: No explicit GDPR Art. 9 consent is captured for health-data processing or AI egress — only OS-level read grants exist
findings.rule: GDPR Art. 9(2)(a) — processing special-category data requires explicit, purpose-specific consent; the source OS/OAuth grant authorizes reading, not the app's downstream processing/sharing.
findings.currentBehavior: The mobile ConnectHealthScreen requests Health Connect permissions and immediately reads/uploads 6 months of history with only a one-line subtitle ('reads your Garmin health history ... to learn your baselines'); no explicit consent checkbox, no privacy-policy link on the screen, and no mention of AI processing/egress. The Garmin web OAuth flow captures Garmin's own consent to read, not the app's Art. 9 processing consent.
findings.evidence: ConnectHealthScreen.tsx:111-136 (permission request → fetch → upload with no explicit consent gate), :140-144 (subtitle only, no purpose limitation / AI disclosure / policy link). integrationsController.js:462-511 (Garmin connect/callback capture OAuth grant only). No 'consent' capture found in the health flow (grep 'consent' matched only Spotify/OAuth surfaces).
findings.remediation: Add an explicit, granular consent step before the first health read: state the purpose (biometric-adaptive music), the retention, any AI sub-processor, and link the privacy policy; store consent + timestamp + version server-side to evidence Art. 9 compliance and to satisfy Google Play Health Connect's in-app disclosure requirement. The OS permission grant alone is not a lawful basis for the app's processing/sharing.
findings.cleanAlternative: A dedicated pre-permission consent screen (purpose, data types, retention, sub-processors, withdrawal path) with an affirmative action, persisted as a versioned consent record; withdrawal wired to the existing erasure cascade.

findings.title: Health time-series and profile data have no retention limit / TTL — indefinite storage of special-category data (GDPR storage-limitation)
findings.rule: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) — storage limitation: special-category data must not be kept longer than necessary.
findings.currentBehavior: BiometricLog is only 'capped at 100k docs per user in application logic' with no time-based expiry, and MedicalProfile is retained indefinitely. Other collections (ServeEvent 90d, RefreshToken) do use TTL indexes, so the omission on the most sensitive data is conspicuous.
findings.evidence: BiometricLog.js:25 comment 'Capped at 100k docs per user' with only index({userId,recordedAt}) at :28 — no expireAfterSeconds. MedicalProfile.js — no TTL index. Contrast ServeEvent.js:26 (expireAfterSeconds: 90*24*3600) and RefreshToken.js:19 (expireAfterSeconds). No expireAfterSeconds match on either health model in the TTL grep.
findings.remediation: Define and enforce a documented retention window for raw biometric time-series (e.g. rolling N months, sufficient for baselines) via a TTL index or scheduled pruning, and document MedicalProfile retention tied to account lifetime. Publish the retention period in the privacy policy.
findings.cleanAlternative: Add a TTL index on BiometricLog.recordedAt sized to the analytics window (baselines are median-aggregated onto MedicalProfile anyway, so raw rows need not persist indefinitely), plus a documented account-lifetime retention for MedicalProfile.

findings.title: No self-service data access/portability (GDPR Art. 15/20) — only erasure is implemented
findings.rule: GDPR Art. 15 (right of access) & Art. 20 (portability); CCPA right to know.
findings.currentBehavior: The app implements erasure (DELETE /auth/account cascade) but exposes no endpoint or flow for a user to obtain a copy of the health/personal data held about them.
findings.evidence: authController.js:220-245 (deleteAccount) + privacy/erasure.js:25-37 (cascade) implement erasure; grep for export/download/data-request in authController and controllers returned no access/portability endpoint.
findings.remediation: Provide a data-access/export path (self-service export or a documented manual DSAR process) covering BiometricLog, MedicalProfile, MusicProfile, PlaylistSession, and account fields, decrypted for the subject. Document the SLA in the privacy policy.
findings.cleanAlternative: A read-only authenticated export endpoint that serializes the user's decrypted records to JSON on demand, reusing the same collection inventory the erasure cascade already enumerates.

findings.title: ADR-0005 audit-trail requirement for biometric decryption is not implemented
findings.rule: ADR 0005 Consequences: 'Any code path that decrypts a biometric field must be auditable (audit-trail requirement).'
findings.currentBehavior: Multiple paths decrypt vitals (resolveBiometricContext, biosonic baselines, profile serialization via getters) with no audit log recording who/what/when a biometric field was decrypted, so the ADR's auditability guarantee is unmet.
findings.evidence: biometricHandler.js:314-345 decrypts profile fields with no audit emit; baselines.js:82 caches encrypted stats but decryption paths are unlogged; encryptedField.js:26-29,40-42 getters decrypt transparently on every read with no trace. ADR 0005 line 18 requires an audit trail.
findings.remediation: Either implement a lightweight decryption audit trail (worker-scope access log for biometric fields) as ADR 0005 mandates, or amend the ADR if the requirement is deemed disproportionate — do not leave the locked decision silently unmet.
findings.cleanAlternative: Route all biometric decryption through a single audited accessor that logs userId + purpose + timestamp (no plaintext values), satisfying the ADR without leaking vitals into logs.

findings.title: Field-level encryption does not bind ciphertext to the user via AAD, despite util + ADR support for context binding
findings.rule: Defense-in-depth / ADR 0005 ('optional AAD context binding'); the encryption util already supports AAD.
findings.currentBehavior: The encryptedNumber/encryptedString setters call encrypt(String(v)) with no AAD, so a biometric ciphertext is not cryptographically bound to its owning user — a swapped/misrouted ciphertext would still decrypt cleanly. The util and ADR both anticipate userId-AAD binding (biosonic baselines.js:82 does pass userId as AAD, showing the pattern is available and used elsewhere).
findings.evidence: encryptedField.js:23-24 & :38-39 (set: (v) => encrypt(String(v)) — no aad passed) vs encryption.js:38-47 (encrypt supports aad) and ADR 0005:19 ('optional AAD context binding'). baselines.js:82 uses encrypt(..., String(userId)) — inconsistent with the model-field encryption.
findings.remediation: Bind health-field ciphertext to the userId via AAD in the encryptedField setter/getter (schema fields can access this._doc.userId at write time), matching the baselines pattern, so ciphertext cannot be transplanted across users. Low urgency but cheap hardening.
findings.cleanAlternative: Pass the owning userId as AAD in encryptedNumber/encryptedString and on decrypt, aligning model-field encryption with the already-AAD-bound Redis baseline cache.

findings.title: Garmin webhook fails OPEN — unauthenticated biometric/medical write to any user when GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset
findings.rule: OWASP A07:2021 Identification & Authentication Failures / A01 Broken Access Control
findings.currentBehavior: The only guard on the public POST /api/integrations/garmin/webhook is `if (process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET && req.query.secret !== process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET) return 401`. When the env var is unset/empty the `&&` short-circuits and the check is skipped entirely, so the endpoint accepts anonymous requests.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:531 (guard) — route registered PUBLIC at backend/app/routes/integrations.js:29, ABOVE `router.use(auth)` (line 36). Handler at lines 536-552 groups the request body `{ <summaryType>: [ { userId: <garminUserId>, ...summary } ] }` by `summary.userId`, resolves each via `User.findOne({ garminUserId: gid, deletedAt: null })`, and calls `garminIngest.ingestSummaries(user._id, items)`. Failing scenario: with GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET unset (plausible — the Garmin Health integration is still pending API approval per project state), an attacker POSTs a crafted summary array carrying a known/enumerated victim garminUserId and no `?secret=`; the guard is bypassed and forged resting-HR / HRV / sleep / SpO2 summaries are written to that victim's MedicalProfile, corrupting medical data and downstream music/health inferences. Contrast the two sibling webhooks that fail CLOSED: backend/app/controllers/webhooksController.js:20-23 (RevenueCat returns 503 when its secret is unset) and backend/app/services/wearable/suunto.js:13-16 (Suunto refuses payloads in production when its secret is unset).
findings.remediation: Fail closed: if GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset, refuse (503) instead of skipping the check; only accept the request when a presented secret matches. Mirror the RevenueCat/Suunto pattern already in the codebase.
findings.cleanAlternative: const secret = process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET; if (!secret) return res.status(503).json({ error: 'garmin webhook not configured' }); if (!timingSafeEqualStr(provided, secret)) return res.status(401).json({ error: 'unauthorized' }); — reuse the timingSafeEqualStr helper from webhooksController.js and read `provided` from a header (see next finding).

findings.title: Garmin webhook shared secret transmitted in the URL query string and compared non-constant-time
findings.rule: OWASP A09:2021 (secrets in URLs → logs) / A07
findings.currentBehavior: The webhook authenticates via `req.query.secret`, so the long-lived shared secret rides in the request URL and is compared with a plain `!==`.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:531 — `req.query.secret !== process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET`. Putting the secret in the URL leaks it into Railway/reverse-proxy access logs, APM traces, and any intermediary — exactly the leakage class the F1 audit already eliminated for the session JWT by moving it out of the connect URL (backend/app/utils/jwt.js:30-38 signConnectToken rationale). The `!==` string compare is also a (minor) timing oracle on the secret, unlike the constant-time digest compare used for the RevenueCat secret at backend/app/controllers/webhooksController.js:8-12. Scenario: the secret appears verbatim in deploy/proxy logs; anyone with log access (or a log-forwarding integration) recovers it and can then forge webhook writes indefinitely.
findings.remediation: Move the secret out of the query string into a request header (e.g. Authorization: Bearer / X-Webhook-Secret) and compare with a constant-time digest comparison.
findings.cleanAlternative: Read the secret from `req.headers` and validate with the existing `timingSafeEqualStr` (SHA-256 digest + crypto.timingSafeEqual) from webhooksController.js, so length/content never leak via URL logs or timing.

findings.title: OAuth connect token (?ct=) authenticates ALL protected routes, not just the connect flow
findings.rule: OWASP A01:2021 Broken Access Control (over-broad credential scope)
findings.currentBehavior: The `?ct=` single-use connect token is verified and its purpose is checked (`oauth-connect`), then it authenticates the request like a full session — for every route behind the auth middleware, not only the OAuth connect endpoints it was minted for.
findings.evidence: backend/app/middleware/auth.js:19-24 accepts `req.query.ct`, verifies it, and requires `payload.purpose === 'oauth-connect'`, then sets `req.user` for the rest of the pipeline (line 58). The same middleware guards high-value routes: GET /api/integrations/spotify/token returns the DECRYPTED Spotify access token (backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:185-202) and DELETE /api/auth/account performs an irreversible GDPR hard-delete (backend/app/routes/auth.js:20). The token is single-use (burned at auth.js:53-56) with a 120s TTL, so exploitation needs an attacker to observe and replay the ct before the legitimate connect navigation spends it — a narrow race over HTTPS — but the credential's privilege scope is far broader than the connect flow requires. Scenario: a ct captured from a proxy/referrer/history within the burn window is replayed once against /api/integrations/spotify/token to exfiltrate the victim's Spotify token, or against /api/auth/account to delete the account.
findings.remediation: Scope the connect token to the OAuth connect endpoints: reject a `purpose:'oauth-connect'` token on any non-connect route.
findings.cleanAlternative: Only enter the ct branch when the request targets a connect path (e.g. `req.path.endsWith('/connect')`), or embed an `aud`/allowed-path claim in signConnectToken (utils/jwt.js:33-38) and assert it in the middleware before setting req.user.

findings.title: JWT verification does not pin the signing algorithm
findings.currentBehavior: All token verification calls omit the `algorithms` allow-list, leaving the accepted-algorithm set implicit.
findings.evidence: backend/app/utils/jwt.js:54-60 — `verifyOauthState`/`verifyToken` call `jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET)` with no `{ algorithms: ['HS256'] }`. These back the session middleware (backend/app/middleware/auth.js:20,31), the OAuth-state/connect-token flows (integrationsController.js), and the socket handshake (backend/app/sockets/index.js:37). Because the secret is a symmetric string, jsonwebtoken defaults the accepted set to HS* and rejects `alg:none`, so the classic RS256→HS256 confusion is not directly reachable here — this is a hardening gap, not an active bypass, but it is the one place the otherwise-rigorous crypto posture leaves an implicit default.
findings.remediation: Pin `{ algorithms: ['HS256'] }` on every jwt.verify call (verifyToken, verifyOauthState) so only the intended MAC algorithm is ever accepted.
findings.cleanAlternative: return jwt.verify(token, process.env.JWT_SECRET, { algorithms: ['HS256'] }); applied uniformly in utils/jwt.js.

findings.title: Garmin webhook authentication fails OPEN when GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset
findings.rule: Fail-closed authentication on all write endpoints
findings.currentBehavior: The public webhook only rejects a bad secret when the env var happens to be set; with it unset the check is skipped entirely and anyone can post biometric summaries.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:531 — `if (process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET && req.query.secret !== process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET) return res.status(401)...`. The route is PUBLIC (routes/integrations.js:29, above `router.use(auth)`). Because the guard is gated on the secret being truthy, when GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is not configured the whole condition short-circuits to false and the handler proceeds unauthenticated — accepting attacker-controlled biometric/medical summaries. Per project memory the Garmin Health API is still 'pending approval', so deploying with the secret unset is a realistic state. This is the classic fail-OPEN pattern (OWASP A07 Identification & Authentication Failures / CWE-305). Contrast the Suunto webhook (services/wearable/suunto.js:14, returns false in production when unset) and the RevenueCat webhook (controllers/webhooksController.js:21, returns 503 when unset), both of which fail CLOSED.
findings.remediation: Fail closed: if GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is undefined in production, reject with 503 (as RevenueCat does) or refuse to boot — never accept the request. Move the secret out of the query string into an Authorization header and compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual over SHA-256 digests (as webhooksController.timingSafeEqualStr already does).
findings.cleanAlternative: Reuse the exact fail-closed + constant-time pattern already in webhooksController.js: `const secret = process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET; if (!secret) return res.status(503).json({error:'not configured'}); if (!timingSafeEqualStr(req.headers.authorization||'', 'Bearer '+secret)) return res.status(401)...`.

findings.title: NoSQL operator injection via summary.userId → cross-user biometric/medical data poisoning
findings.rule: Never pass unvalidated request-body values as Mongo query operands
findings.currentBehavior: The Garmin webhook takes `userId` straight from each summary object in the request body and uses it as the value in a Mongoose findOne filter, so a JSON object with query operators matches an arbitrary victim user whose profile then gets attacker-supplied vitals written to it.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:539 `const gid = summary?.userId;` (only checked for truthiness at :540 `if (!gid) continue;`) then :548 `const user = await User.findOne({ garminUserId: gid, deletedAt: null }).select('_id');`. `gid` is never constrained to a string. A payload like `{"dailies":[{"userId":{"$gt":""},"startTimeInSeconds":...,"restingHeartRateInBeatsPerMinute":42,"timeOffsetHeartRateSamples":{"0":200}}]}` makes the filter `{garminUserId:{$gt:''}}`, matching the first user with any garminUserId. The matched user's `_id` is then passed to garminIngest.ingestSummaries → metricStore.persistMetrics, writing forged heartRate/HRV/SpO2/sleep into that victim's MedicalProfile and BiometricLog. Those values surface on the victim's Pulse screen (pulseController) and steer their music generation (biometricHandler.resolveBiometricContext). OWASP A03 Injection / CWE-943. There is no express-mongo-sanitize in the stack (verified in index.js middleware chain), so operator objects reach Mongoose intact. Reachable unauthenticated when combined with the fail-open finding, or by anyone who obtains the URL secret (see the secret-in-query finding).
findings.remediation: Coerce and validate gid to a string before querying: `if (typeof gid !== 'string' || !gid) continue;` (or `String(gid)`). Apply the same string-coercion discipline used in passwordAuth/tokenService to every request-derived query operand.
findings.cleanAlternative: Reject non-string ids and additionally bound the ingested numeric ranges: `const gid = summary?.userId; if (typeof gid !== 'string' || !gid) continue;`. Optionally add express-mongo-sanitize (or Mongoose's sanitizeFilter:true) as defense-in-depth across the app.

findings.title: Webhook shared secret transmitted in the URL query string (?secret=)
findings.rule: Secrets travel in headers/body, never in the URL
findings.currentBehavior: The Garmin webhook authenticates on `?secret=` in the query string and compares it with a plain non-constant-time `!==`, so the standing secret rides in the URL where proxies, access logs and Referer capture it.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:531 reads `req.query.secret` and compares with `!==`. Query strings are logged by reverse proxies (Railway sits in front — see index.js:32 trust-proxy note), stored in access logs, and can leak via Referer/browser history (CWE-598 Information Exposure Through Query String). The codebase already acknowledges this exact risk: middleware/errorHandler.js:14-16 deliberately strips the query string before sending paths to Sentry precisely because '`?secret=` ... would leak those standing/one-time credentials'. The `!==` comparison is also non-constant-time (CWE-208), unlike the timingSafeEqual used for RevenueCat (webhooksController.js:8-12) and Suunto (suunto.js:33).
findings.remediation: Move the secret to an Authorization/`X-Garmin-Webhook-Secret` header and compare with a constant-time digest equality. If Garmin can only call a fixed URL, prefer an HMAC signature over the raw body (as Suunto does) rather than a bearer secret in the path.
findings.cleanAlternative: Read the secret from a header and compare via the existing helper: `if (!timingSafeEqualStr(req.headers['x-garmin-webhook-secret']||'', secret)) return res.status(401)...` — reusing webhooksController.timingSafeEqualStr, which digest-compares to avoid both timing and length leaks.

findings.title: Server-to-server biometric ingest accepts physiologically impossible values (no upper bounds)
findings.rule: Validate numeric ranges on all ingested biometric values
findings.currentBehavior: The Garmin summary and health-store batch normalizers accept any finite positive number, so a heart rate of 99999 or an HRV of 1e9 is persisted, unlike the live socket/watch paths which clamp to physiological ranges.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/wearable/adapter.js:177 `const isPos = (v) => Number.isFinite(v) && v > 0;` gates normalizeGarminSummaries (:194) and fromOffsetMap (:180) with only a positivity check — no upper bound. normalizeHealthStoreSamples (:135) likewise only drops null/NaN (:145-147). Compare the strict physiological clamps enforced elsewhere: watchHrIngest rejects HR outside 30–230 (integrationsController.js:683) and biometricHandler.isValidReading rejects HR >300 (biometricHandler.js:938-950). Garbage biometrics written via these ingest paths pollute MedicalProfile/BiometricLog, skewing the Pulse DTO and generation baselines. Severity is low on its own (authed push scoped to req.user), but it amplifies the impact of the webhook injection finding, which reaches this same sink with an arbitrary victim's userId.
findings.remediation: Apply per-metric physiological bounds in the normalizers (e.g. HR 30–230, HRV 0–500ms, respiration 4–60brpm, SpO2 50–100, sleep-stage minutes 0–~1440) and drop out-of-range samples, mirroring the live-path validation.
findings.cleanAlternative: Introduce a small `RANGES` table keyed by metric and filter in normalizeGarminSummaries/normalizeHealthStoreSamples: `if (value < RANGES[metric].min || value > RANGES[metric].max) continue;`, reusing the same physiological constants already used by watchHrIngest and isValidReading.

findings.title: BiometricLog grows unbounded — documented "100k cap" is never enforced and there is no TTL
findings.currentBehavior: Every wearable/watch reading is inserted into BiometricLog and never trimmed or expired. Live prod holds 16,555 heart-rate rows for the single existing user.
findings.evidence: BiometricLog.js:25 comment claims 'Capped at 100k docs per user in application logic to bound storage', but no enforcing code exists: writers only insertMany (metricStore.js:54, appleHealth.js:36, suunto.js:50) with no BiometricLog.deleteMany/countDocuments trim anywhere (grep across backend/app returned none). The only index is { userId:1, recordedAt:-1 } (BiometricLog.js:28) — no expireAfterSeconds TTL, unlike ServeEvent.js:26. Live count = 16,555 for 1 user (userId 6a4ed339…05cc); the ~6-month Garmin backfill + ~5-min watch pushes make this monotonic.
findings.remediation: Implement the documented retention limit as a ring-buffer trim on write (delete oldest beyond N per user), and/or add a TTL index (e.g. expireAfterSeconds on recordedAt for a bounded retention window). Correct or delete the misleading model comment so it stops asserting a guarantee the code does not provide.
findings.cleanAlternative: Baselines/aggregates the physiology engine needs (biosonic/baselines.js reads pages of BiometricLog) can be computed and persisted onto MedicalProfile, then raw per-reading rows aged out on a TTL — the same signal without indefinitely retaining raw special-category time-series.

findings.title: Wearable disconnect never purges biometric/health data, and Apple Health / Health Connect / Suunto have no disconnect endpoint at all
findings.rule: GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) data minimization / Art. 17 erasure on withdrawal
findings.currentBehavior: garminDisconnect clears only the token/provider/garminUserId on User; it leaves all historical BiometricLog + MedicalProfile rows in place. Apple Health, Health Connect, and Suunto set wearableProvider on push but expose no disconnect route, so a user cannot revoke them or purge their health data short of deleting the whole account.
findings.evidence: integrationsController.js:514-522 (garminDisconnect) sets wearableProvider/wearableToken/garminUserId = null with no BiometricLog/MedicalProfile deletion. routes/integrations.js:64-78 registers only garmin/disconnect and DELETE watch/token — no apple/health_connect/suunto disconnect. Contrast spotifyDisconnect (integrationsController.js:147-160) which explicitly MusicProfile.deleteOne per the provider data-handling policy (line 153-155).
findings.remediation: Add disconnect endpoints for every wearable provider, and on any wearable disconnect delete (or offer to delete) that user's BiometricLog + reset MedicalProfile — mirroring the Spotify-disconnect data-handling posture for the far more sensitive health category.
findings.cleanAlternative: A single DELETE /integrations/wearable/{provider} handler that clears the credential AND calls the erasure helpers scoped to that user's biometric collections, so withdrawing consent for a wearable actually removes the data it produced.

findings.title: PlaylistSession retained indefinitely — encrypted free-text emotion prompts + biometric snapshots accumulate with no TTL
findings.currentBehavior: One PlaylistSession is written per generation and never expired. Each stores the encrypted contextPrompt (the schema itself calls it 'the most re-identifying field') and an encrypted biometricSnapshot.heartRate. Live: 85 rows for one user, growing one-per-generation forever.
findings.evidence: PlaylistSession.js:16 (contextPrompt encrypted, commented 'most re-identifying field'), :20-23 (biometricSnapshot.heartRate encrypted), :70-75 declares { userId,createdAt } and { llmCacheKey } indexes but NO expireAfterSeconds TTL. Live count = 85 for 1 user, all belonging to the same user (orphan check: 0).
findings.remediation: Add a TTL index (or a periodic pruner) bounding session history to the window the history UI actually needs; the durable behavioral signal already lives in the TTL'd ServeEvent ledger.
findings.cleanAlternative: Keep the non-sensitive trackSummary/feedback for the history feed on a bounded retention, and drop/expire the encrypted contextPrompt + biometricSnapshot once the recalibration window has passed.

findings.title: GDPR erasure omits shadow-buffer Redis keys (buffer:<userId>:*)
findings.currentBehavior: Account deletion purges ledger:/pool:/bio:baseline: Redis keys but not the per-user shadow-buffer keys, which hold precompiled per-user playlists keyed by bio-mood band. They survive erasure until their 30-minute TTL lapses.
findings.evidence: shadowBufferRepo.js:11 keys as `buffer:${userId}:${bioMoodKey}` (set with 30-min TTL, :10/:17). userRedisPurge.js:11-17 patternsFor(userId) only lists ledger:${userId}:*, pool:${userId}:*, bio:baseline:${userId} — buffer:* is absent, so eraseUserChildData (erasure.js:36) leaves it behind.
findings.remediation: Add `buffer:${userId}:*` to userRedisPurge.patternsFor so the right-to-erasure cascade clears it immediately rather than relying on TTL, and add a test asserting every user-scoped Redis key prefix is covered.
findings.cleanAlternative: Derive the purge prefix list from a single shared registry of user-scoped key builders so a new per-user Redis namespace can never be added without also being erased.

findings.title: MusicProfile declares plaintext health fields (restingHeartRate, hrZones) that are encrypted everywhere else
findings.currentBehavior: MusicProfile stores restingHeartRate and hrZones as plaintext Numbers. The identical data is encrypted-at-rest in MedicalProfile and BiometricLog. Currently these fields are null in practice (no writer populates them; live MusicProfile.restingHeartRate = null), so this is a latent inconsistency, but any future write would place special-category health data in the clear.
findings.evidence: MusicProfile.js:63-70 (restingHeartRate: Number plaintext, hrZones plaintext min/max) vs MedicalProfile.js:28-29 which wraps the same metric in encryptedNumber(). MusicProfile is read as a HR source at biometricHandler.js:611 and geminiEngine.js:306/316. musicProfileService.js writes no HR field (grep: 0 matches), so it is null today.
findings.remediation: Either remove the unused restingHeartRate/hrZones fields from MusicProfile (single source of truth = MedicalProfile), or wrap them in encryptedNumber() with toJSON/toObject getters like the other health schemas — before any code path begins writing them.
findings.cleanAlternative: Read resting HR exclusively from the encrypted MedicalProfile and delete the duplicate plaintext fields, eliminating both the drift and the plaintext-at-rest risk.

findings.title: Global caches excluded from erasure can retain user-derived track content (TrackCatalog source='library')
findings.currentBehavior: AudioFeature, TrackEmbedding, and TrackCatalog are deliberately kept out of the erasure cascade because they carry no userId (ADR-0008). TrackCatalog additionally supports source='library' rows seeded from a user's MusicProfile.library — track identities (title/artist/genres) a user listens to. Those would persist post-erasure. Currently benign: live count of source='library' = 0 of 9,080 (all 'global').
findings.evidence: erasure.js:19-24 documents the intentional exclusion; TrackCatalog.js:16-20 defines source enum with 'library' = 'seeded from a user's MusicProfile.library'. Live: trackcatalogs source='library' = 0, source='global' = 9,080 (so no user-derived rows exist right now).
findings.remediation: Confirm with legal/product that library-seeded catalog rows are truly de-identified (no 1:1 linkage back to a single user); if a track can be uniquely tied to one user's library, either strip provenance on erasure or reclassify such rows to 'global' only when shared by ≥N users.
findings.cleanAlternative: Promote a track into the global catalog only once it appears in multiple users' libraries (k-anonymity threshold), so no single user's taste is individually recoverable from the non-erased corpus.

findings.title: Provider account id and push tokens stored in plaintext on the User document
findings.currentBehavior: garminUserId (a stable Garmin account identifier) is stored plaintext and indexed for webhook routing; pushTokens (FCM/APNs device tokens) are stored plaintext. Both are removed on account deletion, but sit unencrypted at rest unlike the OAuth token blobs.
findings.evidence: User.js:33 garminUserId plaintext + index (also duplicated inside the encrypted wearableToken blob per its own comment); User.js:45-50 pushTokens array of plaintext token strings. Encryption helper exists (encryption.js:38) and is used for the token blobs but not these identifiers.
findings.remediation: garminUserId must stay queryable so encryption is impractical — instead store a keyed HMAC/hash for the webhook lookup and keep the raw id only inside the encrypted blob; treat pushTokens as credentials (rotate/expire stale ones) rather than indefinite plaintext.
findings.cleanAlternative: Route the Garmin webhook by a deterministic HMAC(garminUserId) index and decrypt the raw id from wearableToken only when needed, so no bare provider identifier sits in the clear.

findings.title: Production data resides in the default 'test' database
findings.currentBehavior: All 13 app collections (18.9 MB of live user data) live in a database literally named 'test', which is the driver default when the connection string has no explicit database path.
findings.evidence: list-databases returned test (18,976,768 bytes) alongside empty admin/local; all app collections (users, biometriclogs, medicalprofiles, …) are under 'test'. This implies MONGO_URI omits an explicit db name (gdpr-delete.js:11 and app connect use MONGO_URI directly).
findings.remediation: Pin an explicit, non-default database name in MONGO_URI (e.g. /kokonada) so a driver/version default change can never silently split reads/writes across databases, and so ops tooling never confuses prod with a scratch 'test' db.
findings.cleanAlternative: Set the database name explicitly in the connection string and add a startup assertion that the connected db name matches the expected value before serving traffic.

findings.title: Session JWT stored in localStorage — readable by any JS (XSS / third-party-SDK compromise → full account takeover)
findings.currentBehavior: The long-lived session JWT is kept in localStorage under key 'koko-token' and sent as an Authorization: Bearer header, and also seeds the Socket.IO handshake.
findings.evidence: frontend/src/lib/api.ts:9-37 stores the session JWT in localStorage ('koko-token') via getToken/setToken and attaches it as `Authorization: Bearer` (authHeaders, lines 34-37). The same token is passed into the realtime socket handshake at frontend/src/hooks/useSocket.ts:77 (`auth: { token: getToken() }`). localStorage is readable by ANY script on the origin, and the app dynamically injects several third-party scripts that run with full origin privileges: Google GSI (frontend/src/pages/LoginPage.tsx:89-90), Apple ID (frontend/src/pages/LoginPage.tsx:127-128), and the Spotify Web Playback SDK (frontend/src/hooks/useSpotifyPlayer.ts:37-41). Any XSS or a compromise of one of those CDNs can read 'koko-token' and fully impersonate the user. The file header comment (frontend/src/lib/api.ts:1-5) states this was a deliberate workaround for third-party-cookie blocking on the cross-site Vercel↔Railway deploy.
findings.remediation: Keep the session token out of JS-readable storage. Have the backend issue it as an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite=None cookie (use CHIPS/Partitioned so it survives third-party-cookie blocking on the cross-site deploy), or serve the API and SPA from the same registrable domain (custom domain / reverse proxy) so a first-party httpOnly cookie works. If a bearer token must remain client-readable, make the access token short-lived (minutes) with a silent refresh so a stolen copy expires quickly.
findings.cleanAlternative: Backend-set httpOnly + Secure + SameSite=None + Partitioned auth cookie that the SPA never reads; short-lived access token plus rotating refresh token if a bearer scheme is truly unavoidable.

findings.title: Content-Security-Policy is Report-Only (never enforced) and permits 'unsafe-inline' scripts
findings.currentBehavior: The site's only CSP is delivered via the Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only header, which never blocks anything and has no report sink, so it neither enforces nor collects violations.
findings.evidence: frontend/vercel.json:19 sets the header key as `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only` — a report-only policy blocks nothing, and the value (line 20) contains no `report-uri`/`report-to` directive, so violations are not even collected: the policy is inert. There is no enforcing CSP elsewhere (frontend/index.html ships no CSP meta tag). Even if promoted to enforcing, `script-src` on line 20 includes `'unsafe-inline'`, which allows injected inline <script> / event-handler execution and defeats CSP's primary XSS defense. Compounded with the JWT-in-localStorage issue, an injected inline script would execute and be able to read/exfiltrate the session token.
findings.remediation: Rename the header to the enforcing `Content-Security-Policy`. Remove `'unsafe-inline'` from `script-src` and load first-party scripts via per-response nonces (or hashes). Add a working `report-to`/`report-uri` sink and bake in report-only mode briefly BEFORE flipping to enforce.
findings.cleanAlternative: Enforcing `Content-Security-Policy` with `script-src 'self' 'nonce-<per-request>' https://accounts.google.com https://appleid.cdn-apple.com https://sdk.scdn.co` (no 'unsafe-inline') plus a live violation-report endpoint.

findings.title: CSP connect-src/img-src use bare-scheme wildcards (`https:` / `wss:`) — open data-exfiltration channel
findings.currentBehavior: connect-src allows fetch/XHR/WebSocket to any https or wss host; img-src allows any https host.
