findings.evidence: frontend/vercel.json:20 — `connect-src 'self' https: wss:` permits network calls to ANY https or wss origin, and `img-src 'self' data: https:` permits any https image endpoint. If script injection ever occurs, these wildcards hand an attacker an unrestricted channel to beacon the stolen JWT and biometric/HR data to any server (fetch/WebSocket, or an pixel). This removes CSP's value as an anti-exfiltration control. (Currently moot only because the whole policy is Report-Only per the finding above — it must be tightened at the same time enforcement is enabled.)
findings.remediation: Pin connect-src to the exact backend origins and the realtime endpoint, e.g. `connect-src 'self' https://<backend>.up.railway.app wss://<backend>.up.railway.app https://api.spotify.com`. Replace `img-src https:` with the specific album-art / avatar CDNs actually used (e.g. Spotify i.scdn.co, Google avatar hosts) instead of a bare-scheme wildcard.
findings.cleanAlternative: Explicit host allowlist for connect-src and img-src (backend + Spotify/Google media hosts only); no `https:`/`wss:` bare-scheme wildcards.

findings.title: Watch device bearer token exposed to the DOM and system clipboard
findings.provider: kokonada watch device token (Garmin HR ingestion)
findings.currentBehavior: On watch setup the plaintext device token is rendered into the page and written to the clipboard so the user can paste it into the sideloaded watch app.
findings.evidence: frontend/src/components/WatchTokenCard/WatchTokenCard.tsx renders the plaintext watch device token into the DOM (`...{token}` at line 107) and copies it to the system clipboard (`navigator.clipboard.writeText(token)` at line 59). It is held in Redux state `integrations.watchToken` (frontend/src/store/slices/integrationsSlice.ts:32, set at line 82). Positive: it is in-memory only and NOT persisted to localStorage (frontend/src/store/persist.ts persists only the player slice), and it is shown once. Residual risk: this token is a long-lived credential authorizing biometric/HR ingestion for the account; while displayed it is reachable by any XSS/injected script via the DOM or the clipboard API, and it lingers on the clipboard after copy.
findings.remediation: Reduce the window the raw long-lived credential is exposed: auto-clear the displayed token after a short timeout, and clear the clipboard once the watch confirms pairing. Better, replace the copy-the-bearer flow with a short-lived one-time pairing code that the watch exchanges server-side for its own device token, so the long-lived secret never lands in the browser DOM/clipboard.
findings.cleanAlternative: Short-lived one-time pairing code (or QR) that the sideloaded watch app exchanges for a device token via the backend; the long-lived bearer is never rendered or copied in the web client.

findings.title: Garmin biometric webhook fails OPEN when GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset
findings.currentBehavior: The guard is `if (process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET && req.query.secret !== ...)`, so when the env var is empty/unset the check is skipped entirely and the PUBLIC endpoint accepts unauthenticated writes.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:531 — `if (process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET && req.query.secret !== process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET)`; route mounted public at backend/app/routes/integrations.js:29 (`router.post('/garmin/webhook', garminWebhook)`). backend/.env.example ships GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET blank with the comment 'Leave blank to disable the check'. There is NO startup guard forcing it in prod — contrast index.js:45-47 (FRONTEND_URL throws if unset in prod) and services/wearable/suunto.js:14-15 (returns false in production when the secret is unset = fail closed). Garmin Health API approval is still pending per project state, so the endpoint may be deployed with no secret. Impact: an attacker who knows a victim's garminUserId can POST forged biometric/medical summaries (integrationsController.js:548 matches on garminUserId) and poison the medical profile that drives the music engine.
findings.remediation: Make the Garmin webhook default-deny: require the secret and, when NODE_ENV==='production' and GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset, refuse to start (boot assertion) or hard-401 every call — mirroring the Suunto/FRONTEND_URL fail-closed pattern already in the codebase.
findings.cleanAlternative: Add a boot-time assertion in index.js that throws if production and any wearable webhook secret is missing, and switch the handler from `if (secret && mismatch)` to `if (!secret || mismatch) return 401`.

findings.title: Frontend Content-Security-Policy is Report-Only (never enforced) and allows script 'unsafe-inline'
findings.currentBehavior: vercel.json emits only `Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only`, which observes but never blocks; with no report-uri/report-to the violation reports also go nowhere. So the SPA ships with no enforced CSP even though backend index.js:37-38 states 'the browser-facing CSP that matters for XSS is set on the frontend'.
findings.evidence: frontend/vercel.json — the sole CSP header key is 'Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only'; its `script-src` includes `'unsafe-inline'` (and OAuth/SDK CDNs). Being report-only, it provides zero XSS mitigation today.
findings.remediation: Promote to an enforcing `Content-Security-Policy` header after validating in report-only; drop `'unsafe-inline'` from script-src (use per-build nonces or hashes / strict-dynamic); if keeping report-only telemetry add a real report endpoint.
findings.cleanAlternative: Ship an enforcing CSP with strict-dynamic + per-build nonces, and keep Report-Only only as a parallel canary during rollout.

findings.title: Backend Docker image runs the Node process as root (no USER directive)
findings.currentBehavior: The Dockerfile never drops privileges, so the API process (and anything that achieves RCE through it) runs as uid 0 inside the container.
findings.evidence: backend/Dockerfile:1-12 — `FROM node:20-slim` … `CMD ["node", "app/index.js"]` with no `USER` line (the official node images ship a ready-made non-root `node` user that is never selected).
findings.remediation: chown /app to the built-in `node` user and add `USER node` before CMD; consider a read-only root filesystem.
findings.cleanAlternative: Add `RUN chown -R node:node /app` then `USER node`, and run the container with `--read-only` + a tmpfs where writes are needed.

findings.title: Garmin webhook shared secret is passed in the URL query string (?secret=) — leaks into access/proxy/Sentry logs
findings.currentBehavior: The secret that authenticates the webhook is read from req.query.secret, so the full secret appears in every request URL captured by Railway access logs, any reverse proxy, and Sentry breadcrumbs/transaction names.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:531 (`req.query.secret`); public route backend/app/routes/integrations.js:29. Suunto by contrast authenticates via an HMAC signature header (services/wearable/suunto.js:12-34), which never lands in URLs.
findings.remediation: Move authentication to a header (Bearer token) or an HMAC over the raw body as Suunto already does, so the secret is never logged; rotate the current secret afterward.
findings.cleanAlternative: Reuse the HMAC-over-raw-body verification pattern from services/wearable/suunto.js for the Garmin webhook.

findings.title: Multi-GB data dumps and nested git worktrees are untracked but NOT gitignored (accidental-commit / secret-copy risk)
findings.currentBehavior: `git status` shows backend/acousticbrainz-*.tar.zst (~10GB), backend/wave1_seed_dump.ndjson (38MB), and full nested worktree checkouts (.worktree-spotify-guard/, mobile/KokonadaHealth/.worktree-onboarding/) as untracked-AND-stageable. A `git add -A` would stage them; the pre-commit hook does not block them (it only rejects apk/aab/keystore/jks/p12/so + a narrow secret regex). Nested worktrees are the sharp edge because developers copy real config/secrets into them per project practice.
findings.evidence: .gitignore has no rule for *.zst / *.ndjson / .worktree-*; git status --short lists the four large artifacts and both .worktree-* dirs as `??`; git check-ignore returns nothing for them; .githooks/pre-commit:10 filter list omits them.
findings.remediation: Add ignore rules: `/backend/*.tar.zst`, `/backend/wave1_seed_dump.ndjson` (or `*.ndjson`), `/.worktree-*`, `**/.worktree-*`, plus the loose onboarding-*.png / *logcat*.txt artifacts; extend the pre-commit guard with a size/path check.
findings.cleanAlternative: Keep bulk dumps in a data/ dir referenced by env and place `git worktree add` targets OUTSIDE the repo root so they can never be staged.

findings.title: Garmin webhook secret compared with non-constant-time `!==` (timing side-channel)
findings.currentBehavior: The Garmin secret is compared with a plain `!==` string comparison, unlike every other secret comparison in the codebase which uses constant-time equality.
findings.evidence: backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:531 uses `req.query.secret !== process.env.GARMIN_WEBHOOK_SECRET`; contrast the timingSafeEqualStr helper at backend/app/controllers/webhooksController.js:8-11 and crypto.timingSafeEqual at services/wearable/suunto.js:33.
findings.remediation: Compare with crypto.timingSafeEqual over digests, or reuse the existing timingSafeEqualStr helper.
findings.cleanAlternative: Import timingSafeEqualStr from webhooksController and use it for the Garmin comparison.

findings.title: docker-compose Mongo & Redis run unauthenticated with host-published ports
findings.rule: no unauthenticated datastores on published ports
findings.currentBehavior: The dev compose starts mongo:7 with only MONGO_INITDB_DATABASE (no root user/password) and redis with no requirepass, both publishing 27017/6379 to all host interfaces. On any non-loopback host this is an open database. It is a dev-only file (prod uses Atlas + Railway Redis), hence low severity.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.yml — mongo service `ports: 27017:27017` with environment only `MONGO_INITDB_DATABASE`; redis service `ports: 6379:6379`, `command: redis-server --appendonly yes` with no `requirepass`.
findings.remediation: Bind publishes to loopback (`127.0.0.1:27017:27017`), set MONGO_INITDB_ROOT_USERNAME/PASSWORD and a Redis requirepass, or drop the port publishes and use the compose network only.
findings.cleanAlternative: Loopback-only binds plus credentials sourced from a local .env, clearly documented as dev-only.

findings.title: Android debug.keystore committed to the repo
findings.currentBehavior: The Android debug keystore is tracked in git. It is the standard debug signing key (well-known password 'android'), so real risk is minimal, but it is a grandfathered exception to the pre-commit hook which now explicitly blocks *.keystore.
findings.evidence: git ls-files → mobile/KokonadaHealth/android/app/debug.keystore (2257 bytes); .githooks/pre-commit:10 blocks *.keystore/*.jks going forward.
findings.remediation: Remove it from tracking and gitignore **/*.keystore, **/*.jks (debug keystores regenerate automatically). Verify it is a DEBUG (not release) key — a release signing key must never be committed.
findings.cleanAlternative: gitignore all keystores and let the Android build regenerate the debug key locally.

findings.title: No CI secret-scanning backstop; the secret pre-commit hook is per-clone opt-in
findings.currentBehavior: Secret prevention relies on each developer having run `git config core.hooksPath .githooks` and ideally having gitleaks installed (the regex fallback is narrow). CI has no gitleaks/trufflehog job, so nothing authoritative catches a secret that slips past a dev machine or a `--no-verify` commit.
findings.evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml has no secret-scan job (only lint/test/build/deploy); .githooks/pre-commit:16-28 makes gitleaks optional and falls back to a narrow added-lines regex.
findings.remediation: Add a gitleaks (or trufflehog) GitHub Actions job on push/PR as the authoritative backstop and keep the pre-commit hook as the fast local gate.
findings.cleanAlternative: Enable GitHub secret scanning + push protection at the repo level plus a gitleaks CI job.

findings.title: Special-category biometric/health vitals (HRV, SpO2, sleep stages, body battery, readiness, HR) are sent in plaintext to Groq and Google Gemini in the emotion prompt
findings.rule: PRIVACY_DECLARATIONS.md: 'Health data is never shared with third parties'; ADR-0005 zero-knowledge biometrics; GDPR Art. 9 special-category data
findings.currentBehavior: On every emotion/mood generation, resolveBiometricContext() decrypts the user's MedicalProfile vitals and hands them to the LLM prompt, which is POSTed to Groq (prod) or Google Gemini.
findings.evidence: backend/app/sockets/biometricHandler.js:301-351 resolveBiometricContext reads decrypted profile.hrv, profile.spO2, profile.bodyBattery, profile.dailyReadiness, profile.sleepStages{deep,light,rem}, restingHeartRate; passed into buildEmotionPlaylist at :596-604. backend/app/services/geminiEngine.js:231-255 _formatBiometric renders 'HRV {n} ms; body battery {n}/100; readiness {n}/100; SpO2 {n}%; last sleep {deep}m deep...'; :286 injects it as 'Last-24h biometric snapshot' into the prompt; :46-91 _generate POSTs the prompt to Groq/Gemini. Directly contradicts docs/PRIVACY_DECLARATIONS.md:24,37,41,52 ('never shared with third parties') and ADR-0005. The prompt-builder comment at geminiEngine.js:261 ('No PII is included') is inaccurate for special-category health data.
findings.remediation: Stop sending raw vitals to any third-party LLM. If contextual health steering is required, either (a) send ONLY the deterministic coarse stateLabel (computeStateVector already produces e.g. 'recovering'/'energized') with no numeric HRV/SpO2/sleep, or (b) gate behind explicit, revocable user consent AND add Groq + Google as named health-data sub-processors in the DPA/privacy policy and correct the App Store/Play declarations before any store submission.
findings.cleanAlternative: moodDescriptors/orchestrator already compute biosonic target bands (bpm/energy/valence) deterministically server-side; feed the LLM only the abstract mood + a single coarse state label, and compute BPM/energy adjustments from vitals locally — the numeric vitals never leave the server.

findings.title: User free-text emotional notes (contextPrompt/textPrompt) are sent verbatim to Groq/Google Gemini
findings.currentBehavior: The user's typed 'how are you feeling' note is passed unmodified into the LLM prompt and sent to the third-party model. Free text can contain names, locations, medical details, or other PII.
findings.evidence: backend/app/sockets/biometricHandler.js:1052-1058 caches state.lastTextPrompt from the emotion_update event; :600 passes it to buildEmotionPlaylist. backend/app/services/geminiEngine.js:284 embeds it as 'User note: "${textPrompt}"' in the prompt POSTed to Groq/Gemini (:46-91). It is also persisted (encrypted) as PlaylistSession.contextPrompt. No consent disclosure ties this free text to third-party LLM processing, and PRIVACY_DECLARATIONS.md:24 asserts no third-party data sharing.
findings.remediation: Disclose third-party LLM processing of user free-text and obtain consent; optionally run a PII scrub before egress. At minimum add Groq/Google to the sub-processor list.
findings.cleanAlternative: Parse intent from the free text server-side into the structured mood/activity signals the pipeline already uses, and send only those derived tokens to the LLM rather than the raw note.

findings.title: YouTube API data (video titles + channel/artist names) is sent to Groq for music-vs-non-music classification
findings.rule: YouTube API Services Developer Policies (restrictions on processing YouTube API data with third-party AI/ML)
findings.currentBehavior: During YouTube library classification/purge, ambiguous items' raw titles and channel names are batched into a Groq prompt as a tie-breaker.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/musicClassifier.js:66-78 _groqNonMusicIndices builds lines from String(t.name) (YouTube video title) and String(t.artist) (channel) and calls llmClient.generateJson; invoked from classifyTracks at :135-139. This is a DISTINCT third-party-AI egress from the known Spotify->Groq HALT. Notably, the sibling discovery caption service was compliance-locked to feature-only for exactly this reason (backend/app/services/discovery/captionService.js:10-13, 66-70), but the classifier and estimator were not.
findings.remediation: Route through compliance-auditor against current YouTube Developer Policies before this ships to YouTube-connected users; if disallowed, rely solely on the deterministic classifier and pool the residue.
findings.cleanAlternative: classifyByMetadata (backend/app/services/musicClassifier.js:43-62) already decides music vs non-music from categoryId/topicDetails/channel/lexicon signals with no LLM; keep ambiguous items in the unclassified pool for later deterministic retry instead of sending titles to Groq.

findings.title: YouTube (and other unmeasured) track title + artist + genres are sent to Groq for audio-feature estimation
findings.currentBehavior: For tracks the measured-features API can't serve (YouTube-only recordings), the LLM estimator prompt includes each track's title, artist, and genres.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/features/llmEstimatorAdapter.js:45-60 _buildPrompt emits '"{t.title}" by {t.artist} [genres: ...]' and calls llmClient.generateJson at :92; the estimator is documented as the youtube:<id> feature path (backend/app/controllers/integrationsController.js:753-754). Same third-party-AI-content concern as the classifier, for a different code path.
findings.remediation: Compliance review of sending provider-identifying track metadata to Groq; if constrained, drop the title/artist line.
findings.cleanAlternative: The prompt already carries a numeric genre-anchor table (llmEstimatorAdapter.js:29-39); estimate from the track's genre tags alone (joined by index) without echoing title/artist to the model.

findings.title: Cross-provider egress: YouTube-derived track titles/artists are sent to the Spotify Search API to resolve playback URIs
findings.currentBehavior: YouTube-sourced tracks are parsed into (title, artist) and queried against Spotify /search so playback can happen on Spotify. One provider's library content is mixed into another provider's API.
findings.evidence: backend/app/services/crossPlatform.js:57-111 translateToSpotify parses YouTube title/channel (parseYouTubeTitle :30-42) and calls spotify.searchTrackUri; backend/app/services/spotify.js:518-543 sends 'track:"{title}" artist:"{artist}"' to GET /search. Called from the generation pipeline at backend/app/sockets/biometricHandler.js:715-717.
findings.remediation: Confirm both Spotify and YouTube developer terms permit cross-provider resolution; it is arguably user-directed for playback, but should be documented in the data-flow/ToS mapping and privacy policy.
findings.cleanAlternative: Document this as user-directed playback resolution (only title/artist strings, no user identifiers leave), and keep it gated to the Spotify-playback path it already is.

findings.title: Long-lived session JWT is stored in browser localStorage (and may ride in URL query params), widening the token-theft surface
findings.currentBehavior: The web client persists the session JWT in localStorage and attaches it as a Bearer header; the header comment also notes it can be sent as a query param on the cross-site deploy.
findings.evidence: frontend/src/lib/api.ts:7-19 setToken/getToken read/write localStorage['koko-token']; :1-5 comment states the JWT is also sent 'as a Bearer header / query param'. authController.js:190 already acknowledges XSS-capture risk ('captured (e.g. from localStorage via XSS)'). A JWT in localStorage is readable by any injected script; a JWT in a URL can leak via server logs, browser history, and Referer headers.
findings.remediation: Prefer the HTTP-only auth cookie for web sessions and avoid placing the long-lived JWT in localStorage or URLs; where a cookie is impossible (native), keep the token in secure storage with short TTL + refresh rotation (already implemented for mobile via tokenService).
findings.cleanAlternative: On web, rely on the existing SameSite HTTP-only cookie set by setAuthCookie; reserve the body/localStorage token strictly for native clients that use OS secure storage, and use the short-lived single-use ?ct= connect token (already implemented) for any top-level navigation instead of the session JWT.
